1: The penalty chapter (Leviticus 20) is the enforcement counterpart to the prohibition chapter (Leviticus 18). Where Leviticus 18 lists the forbidden sexual relations and practices, Leviticus 20 assigns specific penalties. The chapter is addressed again to benei Yisrael — the full community, not just the priests.
2: Ish ish mibenei Yisrael umin-hager hagar beYisrael asher yiten mizaro laMolech mot yumat: any Israelite or sojourner in Israel who gives his children to Molech (laMolech) shall be put to death. Molech is a deity associated with child sacrifice — the name may derive from melek (king) or from the offering term molech/mulk (a type of vow-sacrifice in Punic inscriptions). The tofet (furnace/hearth) in the Hinnom Valley outside Jerusalem (Jeremiah 7:31) was the site of Molech-child sacrifice. The stoning penalty for Molech worship is carried out by am haaretz (the people of the land) — communal enforcement rather than judicial execution alone.
3: Va-ani etten et-panai ba-ish hahu vehichratti oto miqqerev ammo: and I will set my face against that man and cut him off (karet) from among his people. The divine face-setting against Molech worshipers introduces both human penalty (stoning) and divine penalty (karet = being cut off). Karet (cutting off) is the distinctive Levitical divine penalty — distinct from human-executed punishment, it describes premature death or exclusion from covenant community at the divine level. Giving children to Molech is specifically described as defiling YHWH's sanctuary and profaning his holy name — the theological gravity matches the penalty's severity.
4: Ve-im haalem yaaleimu am haaretz et-eineihem min-ha-ish hahu: if the people of the land at all close their eyes (haalem yaaleimu, an emphatic infinitive absolute) to that man. Social complicity — willful ignorance of a covenant violation — is itself punishable. The community that fails to act against Molech worship shares in its guilt. The communal enforcement mechanism built into the Holiness Code makes every Israelite a stakeholder in covenant maintenance.
5: Vesamti ani et-panai ba-ish hahu uvemishpachto vehichratti oto: I myself will set my face against that man and his family and cut him off. The divine penalty extends to the family — not because relatives are automatically guilty but because participation in Molech worship was typically a family affair, and the community of the household shares in its head's apostasy.
6: Vehanefesh asher tifneh el-haoboth veel-hayideonim liznot achareihem: the person who turns to mediums (ovoth) and necromancers (yideonim) to prostitute himself by following them. The language of spiritual prostitution (liznot) connects occult consultation with idolatry — both are covenant infidelity. The penalty is karet — divine cutting off.
7: Vehitqaddashtem vihyitem qedoshim ki ani YHWH eloheichem: consecrate yourselves and be holy, for I am YHWH your God. The holiness refrain (cf. Leviticus 19:2) appears here at the center of the penalty chapter, reminding that the penalties serve a constructive purpose: they protect the holiness of the community. The penalties are not merely punitive but purgative — removing the corruption that threatens the integrity of the covenant people.
8: Ushmartem et-chuqqotai vaasitem otam ani YHWH meqaddishchem: keep my statutes and do them — I am YHWH who sanctifies you (meqaddishchem). YHWH is the agent of Israel's sanctification: Israel does not make itself holy through observance but is made holy by YHWH. The keeping of statutes is the human response to divine sanctification, not its cause.
9: Ki-ish ish asher yeqallel et-aviv veet-immo mot yumat: anyone who curses his father or mother shall be put to death. The capital penalty for cursing parents places parental honor at the level of covenant seriousness — consistent with the fifth commandment's position in the Decalogue. Aviv veimmo qillel: he has cursed his father and his mother — his blood is upon him (damo bo). The self-responsibility formula (damo bo) recurs throughout the penalty chapter, indicating that the condemned person bears the moral weight of their own execution.
10: Veish asher yinaf et-eshet ish asher yinaf et-eshet reehub: a man who commits adultery with the wife of his fellow. Both the adulterer and the adulteress shall be put to death. The symmetry of the penalty — both parties equally liable — reflects a significant level of gender equity in the judicial framework compared to some ANE law codes that punished only the woman. The equal penalty affirms that both parties acted as moral agents and both bear equal responsibility.
11: Veish asher yishkav et-eshet aviv: the man who lies with his father's wife. This covers both the man and the woman: both shall be put to death, their blood is upon them. The penalty section (Leviticus 20:10-21) applies capital punishment or karet to the forbidden relations listed in Leviticus 18, working through the same categories: adultery (v10), father's wife (v11), daughter-in-law (v12), male homosexual intercourse (v13), and so on.
12: Veish asher yishkav et-khalato: a man who lies with his daughter-in-law — both shall be put to death. Tevel (confusion, perversion) is the term for this violation — the same word used in Leviticus 18:23 for bestiality. The term reflects the Levitical categorization: these acts mix what should be kept distinct (parent/child-in-law roles; human/animal categories).
13: Ve-ish asher yishkav et-zachar mishkevei isha: a man who lies with a male as with a woman — both have committed an abomination (to'evah). The same term (to'evah) and the same capital penalty as in Leviticus 18:22. The context of the penalty chapter's systematic treatment of all forbidden relations shows this is not a marginal concern in the Holiness Code but is treated as seriously as adultery and incest.
14: Veish asher yiqqach et-isha veet-imma zimmah hi: a man who takes a woman and her mother — it is depravity (zimmah). All three shall be burned (basesh yisrefu) — the distinctive penalty of burning (rather than stoning) is reserved for the most egregious cases (cf. Leviticus 21:9 for a priest's daughter). The severity of the penalty reflects the severity of the violation: multiple kinship bonds collapsed simultaneously.
15: Veish asher yitten shekhavto bivehemah: a man who lies with an animal shall be put to death, and you shall kill the animal as well. The animal's execution (despite its lack of moral agency) serves to remove the instrument of the violation from the community — a practice paralleled in the case of a goring ox (Exodus 21:28-29). The animal is a davar nevelah (something unclean); its removal prevents any further use of it that would perpetuate the defilement.
16: Ve-isha asher tiqrav el kol-behemah lerivatah otah: and a woman who approaches any animal to lie with it — you shall kill the woman and the animal. The equal treatment of male and female in the bestiality penalties is consistent with the chapter's pattern of bilateral moral accountability.
17: Veish asher yiqqach et-achoto bat-aviv o vat-immo: a man who takes his sister — daughter of his father or daughter of his mother. The penalty is karet rather than execution, suggesting a different level of severity even within the category of forbidden unions. Chesed hu (it is a disgrace/shame) — chesed in this unusual sense means shameful lovingkindness (the word's positive meaning inverted) — some render it reproach or disgrace.
18: Veish asher yishkav et-isha davah: a man who lies with a woman during her menstrual impurity — both shall be cut off (nikretu) from among their people. The mutual karet penalty for deliberate violation of the niddah restriction is lighter than execution but still serious. The bilateral penalty again emphasizes mutual moral agency.
19: Veervet achat imecha veervet achat avicha lo tegalleh: you shall not uncover the nakedness of your mother's sister or your father's sister — it would uncover one's own flesh (sheer). The karet penalty applies. The aunt relationships involve not just the direct kin but the extension of the kin-network into which one is embedded.
20: Veish asher yishkav et-dodato: a man who lies with his uncle's wife. This case does not receive the execution penalty but instead: chataam yis'u (they shall bear their punishment/sin), and they shall die childless (aririn yamuto). The death-without-children penalty is understood as a divine curse rather than a judicial execution — a form of the Holiness Code's awareness that covenant violations bring natural consequences, not only judicial ones.
21: Veish asher yiqqach et-eshet achiv niddah hi: a man who takes his brother's wife — it is impurity (niddah). The same consequence: they shall die childless. This is the reverse of the levirate marriage institution (Deuteronomy 25:5-10) where marrying a deceased brother's wife was required. The different treatment reflects whether the brother is alive (here: forbidden, treated as adultery) or dead without children (Deut 25: commanded to preserve the family name).
22: Ushmartem et-kol-chuqqotai veet-kol-mishpatai vaasitem otam: keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them. The land-vomiting warning reappears: velo taqii etchem haaretz asher ani meivi etchem shamah lashevet ba (so that the land to which I am bringing you to dwell there does not vomit you out). The land is personified as a moral participant that cannot tolerate persistent covenant violation — the same imagery as Leviticus 18:28.
23: Velo telechu bechukot hagoy asher anochi meshalleach mipeneichem: you shall not walk in the statutes of the nation that I am sending out before you. The Canaanites' expulsion from the land is attributed to their practice of the very offenses listed in the chapter — not ethnic inferiority but covenant-violation resulted in their removal. This theology prevents Israel from claiming special treatment: the same standard applies to them if they commit the same violations.
24: Va-omar lachem atem tirbu et-admatam va-ani atenah lachem la-reshet otah eretz zavat chalav udevash: I said to you that you would inherit their land — a land flowing with milk and honey. The promise of the land is grounded in the distinction YHWH made between Israel and the nations: va-avdil etchem min-haamim lihyot li (I have separated you from the peoples to be mine). The separation theology of the Holiness Code serves an election purpose: Israel's distinctiveness is not superiority but vocation — to be YHWH's covenant people.
25: Vahivdaltem bein habehemah hatehorah letemeah: you shall therefore separate (havdil) the clean from the unclean beast and between unclean and clean birds. The havdalah (separation, distinction) that structures the purity laws is the same cognitive and ethical act demanded in all of Leviticus: maintaining the distinctions that YHWH established at creation. Velo tesha'qetzu et-nafshoteichem: you shall not defile yourselves through the animals. The connection between dietary separation and holiness-separation is explicit here.
26: Vihyitem li qedoshim ki qadosh ani YHWH veavdil etchem min-haamim lihyot li: you shall be holy to me, for I YHWH am holy, and I have separated you from the peoples to be mine. The holiness refrain reaches its fullest expression here: the source of the command (YHWH's holiness), the content (separation from the nations), and the purpose (to belong to YHWH) are all stated together. The closing verse of the penalty chapter's theological section presents holiness as both inherited (YHWH's character) and achieved (Israel's calling).
27: Ve-ish o isha ki yihyeh bahem ov o yideoni mot yumatu: a man or woman who is a medium (ov) or wizard (yideoni) shall be put to death — stoned, their blood is upon them. The chapter ends with the execution penalty for the two main forms of occult practice (cf. Leviticus 19:31), bracketing the chapter with the theme of unauthorized access to spiritual knowledge. The penalties for Molech worship (Leviticus 20 opening) and occult practice (Leviticus 20 closing) frame all the sexual and kinship prohibitions between them, suggesting that all three — child sacrifice, sexual violations, and necromancy — are expressions of the same fundamental rejection of YHWH's covenant order.
